name: Krasue
description: Thai floating head Ghost - 'กระสือ' (krase). Head detaches and Flows with trailing Organs (entrails). Famous in Thai horror. Lived normal human life before - became Krasue after death. Active at Night - seeks blood/raw flesh. Whistling cry is signature. Warnings: used to warn pregnant women (fear maternal death). Cursed being - eternally Hungry. Daytime: appears normal, lives normal. Night: transforms. Can appear also in Laos ('Krasue'), Cambodia ('Krasu'). Connected to feminine vulnerability and death.
appearance: Beautiful young woman's Head - wide eyes, long black flowing hair. NO Body - intestines/organs dangle Beneath like rope/guts. May Glow. Sometimes bird-like flapping sounds. Terrifying contrast: beautiful face, horrible underneath.
behavior: Floats through air. Makes/whistling Crying sounds. Seeks blood/raw flesh at night. Enters houses. Causes illness if touches. Normal life by day - no memory of night acts. Terrorize communities. Can be Repelled by glass beads, certain herbs.
habitat: Roads, fields, cemeteries, banana groves at night
cultural_significance: Thai/Laos/Cambodian FLYING HEAD tradition. Explains sudden deaths, mysterious illnesses especially to pregnant women. Popular in horror media. CONNECTED to fear of maternal/infant mortality.
summary: Krasue is a ghost from the folklore and mythology of Thailand.
fun_fact: Thai floating head GHOST - 'กระสือ' (krase). HEAD detaches and FLOWS with trailing ORGANS (entrails). Famous in Thai horror. Lived normal human life b
seo_description: Thai floating head GHOST - 'กระสือ' (krase). HEAD detaches and FLOWS with trailing ORGANS (entrails). Famous in Thai horror. Lived normal human life b
source: Thailand mythology, oral tradition & folklore collections

name: Kratt
description: Kratt is an artificial creature from Estonian folklore, a construct brought to life through a pact with the Devil. A kratt is created from found objects such as hay, straw, old household implements, or wooden tools by a person who makes a deal with the dark powers, typically by offering three drops of blood. Once animated, the kratt becomes an tireless servant that performs any task commanded of it, from household chores to stealing goods from neighbors. The belief in kratid reflects the Estonian folk tradition of resourceful peasants creating supernatural helpers to improve their lot, though always at a spiritual cost. The kratt is a morally ambiguous being, neither good nor evil in itself, but its creation requires a transaction with evil that inevitably corrupts the maker. The legend serves as a cautionary tale about the dangers of seeking shortcuts to wealth and comfort
appearance: A kratt has no fixed form but is cobbled together from whatever materials its creator has at hand, resulting in a rough, patchwork appearance that is unmistakably artificial. The most common base is a pile of hay or straw twisted into a crude humanoid shape, with sticks or old broom handles for arms and legs tied on with twine. The creators may add old clothing, rags, or a sack for a head, sometimes drawing crude eyes and mouth with charcoal. Some kratid are made from broken tools, wagon wheels, or barrel staves lashed together, creating a clattering, rattling construct that moves with jerky, mechanical motions. Despite its crude construction, the kratt moves with surprising speed and purpose, and it can transform itself into different shapes as commanded, including taking on a human-like appearance to perform tasks such as visiting the market. The most distinctive feature of any kratt is that it casts no shadow and leaves no footprints, betraying its supernatural origins
behavior: A kratt is a tireless worker that never sleeps, eats, or tires, performing any task assigned by its master without question or complaint. It is most commonly used for stealing, flying invisibly to neighboring farms and returning with grain, livestock, milk, or money. The kratt can also perform household chores, farm work, and construction at supernatural speed. However, the creature has a critical flaw: it must always be given a task. If its master runs out of commands, the kratt will turn on its creator, destroying property, harming livestock, or attacking the master themselves. The only way to stop a rampaging kratt is to give it an impossible task such as carrying water in a sieve or building a staircase to the sky, which causes it to fly upward in a rage and burn up as a meteor-like bolide. A kratt can be destroyed by fire, by learning its secret name and commanding it to disassemble, or by driving an iron nail through its heart. The creators soul is forfeit to the Devil upon death
habitat: Homes, wherever master directs
cultural_significance: Estonian magical tradition. Related to Germanic Kobold. Featured in Estonian literature and film "November."
summary: Estonian artificial creature made from hay and implements, brought to life through a pact with the Devil to perform chores
fun_fact: Estonian artificial creature made from hay and implements, brought to life through a pact with the Devil to perform chores
seo_description: Kratt is an artificial creature from Estonian folklore created from hay and household objects through a pact with the Devil. These tireless servants steal for their masters but must be kept busy or they turn violent and destructive
source: Estonian folklore; an artificial creature made from household items and animated by a contract with the devil.

name: Krishna
description: Krishna is one of the most revered deities in Hinduism, worshipped as the eighth avatar of Vishnu and as the Supreme God in his own right within the Vaishnava tradition. He is the central figure of the Bhagavad Gita, where he serves as the divine charioteer and spiritual guide to the warrior Arjuna on the battlefield of Kurukshetra, revealing profound philosophical truths about duty, devotion, and the nature of reality. Krishna is known by many names and aspects: the divine cowherd who charmed the gopis with his flute playing in Vrindavan, the mischievous child who stole butter, the slayer of demons, and the king of Dwarka. He embodies divine love, compassion, and joy, and his teachings in the Bhagavad Gita form the philosophical foundation of much of Hindu thought. The Krishna tradition is one of the largest and most influential within Hinduism
appearance: Krishna is most famously depicted with blue or dark blue skin, representing his divine nature and cosmic vastness, wearing a peacock feather crown and a yellow silk dhoti that gleams like gold. He is often shown playing a bamboo flute, the sound of which is said to captivate all living beings and represent the divine call to the soul. His form is youthful and graceful, with a gentle smile, large lotus-like eyes, and a peacock feather always adorning his head. He wears elaborate gold jewelry including necklaces, armlets, and anklets, and carries a flower garland that reaches his knees. In his four-armed form as Vishnu, he holds a conch shell, discus, mace, and lotus flower. In the Bhagavad Gita, he reveals his universal form to Arjuna as a cosmic being with countless faces, eyes, and mouths, radiating the light of a thousand suns simultaneously, a form so magnificent and terrifying that Arjuna trembles before it
behavior: Krishna actions throughout his divine life illustrate the Hindu concept of lila, or divine play, where the god engages with the world out of love and joy rather than necessity. As a child, he performed miracles and playful pranks such as stealing butter, teasing the gopis, and hiding the clothes of bathing maidens. As a youth, he charmed the cowherd women of Vrindavan with his flute playing, engaging in divine love play that symbolizes the souls longing for God. As an adult, he served as a diplomat, strategist, and king, guiding the Pandavas to victory in the Mahabharata war through a combination of divine wisdom and practical cunning. His relationship with his devotee Arjuna exemplifies the ideal of prapatti or complete surrender to God. Followers of Krishna engage in bhakti yoga, the path of loving devotion, through chanting his names, singing his praises, and meditating on his divine form and pastimes
habitat: Vrindavan, various
cultural_significance: One of the most worshipped Hindu gods
summary: Major Hindu deity, eighth avatar of Vishnu, who revealed the Bhagavad Gita and embodies divine love, joy, and cosmic wisdom
fun_fact: Krishna is the blue-skinned Hindu deity worshipped as the eighth avatar of Vishnu and the Supreme God. He revealed the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna, charmed all with his flute playing, and embodies divine love, joy, and cosmic wisdom
seo_description: Krishna is the blue-skinned Hindu deity worshipped as the eighth avatar of Vishnu and the Supreme God. He revealed the Bhagavad Gita to Arjuna, charmed all with his flute playing, and embodies divine love, joy, and cosmic wisdom
source: Hindu mythology, Vedas, Puranas & Mahabharata

name: Krsnik
description: Krsnik is a vampire hunter from Slovenian and Croatian folklore, a special individual born with supernatural abilities that enable them to see, track, and destroy vampires and other undead creatures. The name krsnik derives from krs meaning cross or baptism, reflecting the Christian folk beliefs mixed with older pagan traditions that characterize these warriors. Krsniks are believed to be born with a caul or born on a Saturday, signs that mark them as chosen for this sacred duty. They operate at the boundary between the human world and the supernatural, possessing spiritual vision that allows them to see vampires in their true forms even when they appear as ordinary people. The krsnik tradition represents a unique blend of Christian and pre-Christian Slavic beliefs about the undead, combining Church-sanctioned methods such as holy water and crosses with older folk magic
appearance: A krsnik appears as an ordinary person, deliberately maintaining a plain appearance to avoid drawing attention from the supernatural enemies they hunt. They typically dress in simple, practical clothing suitable for their region, carrying a small bag of tools that includes a wooden stake, a hammer, a vial of holy water, a cross or crucifix, garlic braids, and a small knife inscribed with protective symbols. In ceremonial contexts, a krsnik may wear a red sash or belt that marks their office, and some carry a candle blessed by a priest that burns with a flame only they can see. Experienced krsniks often bear the subtle marks of their battles: scars from vampire bites, burns from holy water, or a distinctive pallor from the sleepless nights spent hunting. Their eyes are said to be unusually sharp, able to see in near darkness and to perceive the faint glow that surrounds supernatural beings invisible to ordinary sight
behavior: A krsnik spends their life in a constant war against vampires and other undead, using a combination of traditional methods and personal spiritual power. They track vampires by their supernatural signs: livestock that sicken for no reason, unexplained deaths in a village, the appearance of strange lights in graveyards, or the presence of animals associated with the undead. When a vampire is located, the krsnik performs the required rituals to destroy it, which may include staking through the heart, decapitation, burning the remains, or reciting specific prayers and incantations. Some krsniks are also skilled in defensive magic, creating protective charms for villagers, blessing homes against vampire entry, and performing rituals to prevent the dead from rising. The krsnik is sometimes believed to possess a guardian spirit or to be able to leave their physical body during sleep to fight vampires in the spiritual realm. Despite their power, krsniks live in constant danger, as vampires they have failed to destroy will seek revenge
habitat: Slovenian villages, forests
cultural_significance: Slovenian folk hero/vampire hunter. Part of Balkan vampire-hunting tradition.
summary: Slovenian vampire hunter born with supernatural abilities who uses crosses, stakes, and holy water to destroy the undead
fun_fact: Krsnik is a Slovenian vampire hunter born with supernatural abilities to see and destroy the undead. These warriors blend Christian and pagan methods to protect their communities from vampires using stakes, holy water, and ancient protective charms
seo_description: Krsnik is a Slovenian vampire hunter born with supernatural abilities to see and destroy the undead. These warriors blend Christian and pagan methods to protect their communities from vampires using stakes, holy water, and ancient protective charms
source: Slovenian folklore; vampire hunters born with supernatural abilities.

name: Kräkl (or Kratt)
description: Krakl, also spelled Kratt or Kraat, is a artificial being from Estonian folklore, a construct created by sorcerers from natural and household materials and animated through dark magic. Like the more common kratt, the krakl is a servitor created to perform tasks for its master, ranging from everyday chores to supernatural theft. However, the krakl is distinguished by its more rudimentary construction and more dangerous temperament, being considered a cruder and less reliable version of the kratt. The belief in such artificial beings is deeply rooted in Estonian and broader Finno-Ugric folk tradition, where the boundary between the natural and supernatural was fluid, and where clever but morally compromised individuals sought to gain advantage through spiritual deals. The krakl legend reflects the cultural understanding that shortcuts to prosperity come with hidden costs
appearance: The krakl is created from whatever materials are most readily available to its maker, resulting in an even cruder and more grotesque appearance than a kratt. Common constructions include a roughly humanoid shape made from hay, straw, wooden sticks, and old rags tied together with twine or leather strips. Some krakli are made from a single log carved into a crude human shape, with branches left as stumps for arms and legs. The head is often a sack stuffed with hay, with features roughly indicated by charcoal marks or sewn-on buttons for eyes. The creatures limbs are uneven and move with a jerky, uncoordinated quality, and its joints creak and groan as it moves. Some krakli appear as shapeless bundles that roll or crawl rather than walk upright. The creature has no face or voice, but it communicates through gestures and the vibrations of its movements, and the space around it feels cold and wrong
behavior: The krakl works tirelessly at whatever tasks its master commands, performing farm work, household chores, and theft with mechanical efficiency that far exceeds human capability. It cannot speak, but it understands complex commands and carries them out with literal, unthinking obedience. However, the krakl is even more dangerous than a proper kratt because it lacks the intelligence to understand context or nuance. It must be constantly kept busy, as any idle moment will cause it to turn its destructive energy on its surroundings, destroying property, attacking livestock, and harming anyone in its path. The krakl can be destroyed by fire or by the creator revealing its secret name, which causes the creature to collapse into its component parts. It is also vulnerable to iron, and a iron nail driven through its center will render it inanimate permanently. If the creator dies without destroying their krakl, the creature remains active, a dangerous loose end that threatens the entire community
habitat: Farmhouses, barns
cultural_significance: Folkloric version of a golem. Explains mysterious farm work and warnings about dark magic.
summary: Estonian artificial creature created from hay and sticks by a sorcerer, cruder and more dangerous than the proper Kratt
fun_fact: Estonian artificial creature created from hay and sticks by a sorcerer, cruder and more dangerous than the proper Kratt
seo_description: Krakl is a crude artificial creature from Estonian folklore created from hay, sticks, and rags through dark magic. This dangerous construct works tirelessly for its master but turns destructive if left idle or if its creator dies
source: Estonian folklore; variation of the kratt, often described as cruder versions.

name: Kukulkan (Plumed Serpent)
description: The feathered serpent deity of Maya mythology, representing the duality of sky and earth. Kukulkan (feathered snake) taught civilization, astronomy, and agricultural practices. His temples at Chichén Itzá feature the famous equinox serpent descent.
appearance: Kukulkan appears as a magnificent serpent covered in the brilliant iridescent green and blue feathers of the quetzal bird, the most sacred bird in Maya tradition. The serpent body is long and powerful, covered in overlapping scales that shimmer with color, while the feathers form a crest on the head, a mane along the neck, and decorative plumes along the body. At the head, the serpent features a human face or a stylized serpent face with feathered cheek ornaments, reflecting the dual nature of the deity as both earthly serpent and celestial bird. In Maya art from Guatemala, Kukulkan is depicted on temple walls and carved stelae in stylized form, often with the body coiled or undulating, the head in profile with a long snout, and an open mouth from which a human head or deity emerges. The most famous Guatemalan representation is at the site of Tikal, where the Temple of the Serpent features elaborate carvings of Kukulkan
behavior: Kukulkan is a creator deity and culture hero who brought the gifts of civilization to the Maya people, teaching them agriculture, astronomy, mathematics, calendar-making, and the arts that defined classical Maya civilization. As a god of wind and rain, he brings the life-giving rains essential for maize cultivation, sweeping away the dry season to make way for growth. He descends from the heavens to walk among humans, sharing divine wisdom and establishing the proper social and cosmic order. In Guatemala, Kukulkan is particularly associated with the Quiche Maya, where he appears in the Popol Vuh creation epic as the god who helped create humanity and establish the world order. He represents the fundamental duality of existence: sky and earth, spirit and matter, the celestial and the terrestrial. His worship continues among traditional Maya communities in Guatemala today, where he is syncretized with Catholic saints and honored in ceremonies that blend ancient and modern practices
habitat: Temples, celestial realms, forests
cultural_significance: One of the most important Mesoamerican deities. Represents the fusion of serpent (earth) and bird (sky). Connected to Quetzalcoatl of Aztec mythology. The pyramid El Castillo at Chichén Itzá is dedicated to him.
summary: Maya feathered serpent deity of Guatemala who brought civilization and appears in the Popol Vuh creation epic
fun_fact: Kukulkan is the Maya feathered serpent deity of Guatemala who brought civilization, astronomy, and agriculture. Featured in the Popol Vuh creation epic, he bridges heaven and earth and continues to be honored by traditional Maya communities
seo_description: Kukulkan is the Maya feathered serpent deity of Guatemala who brought civilization, astronomy, and agriculture. Featured in the Popol Vuh creation epic, he bridges heaven and earth and continues to be honored by traditional Maya communities
source: Maya mythology; Kukulkan is the feathered serpent deity of wind and rain.
